summary: Gruber reviews the 24-inch M1 iMac, finding it a landmark design achievement enabled by Apple Silicon. He traces the lineage of colorful, desirable Mac hardware back to the 1984 original and the G3 iMacs, arguing these new models recapture that spirit while looking unmistakably modern. The external power supply, ultra-thin profile, and vibrant color options make it feel more like a beautiful display than a traditional desktop computer. Performance matches other M1 Macs, which is to say everything feels instantaneous. He addresses the white bezel controversy (it disappears in use), the surprising retention of the chin (it makes sense structurally), and questions why Apple chose Touch ID over Face ID. He concludes by declaring it an iconic design that will stand the test of time.
key_points: The M1 iMac's 50% volume reduction and ultra-thin profile make it look like a display rather than a computer, enabled by the same chip efficiency that powers the fanless MacBook Air.
The external power supply is a deliberate design 'cheat' that Apple has used before, and hiding ungainly components out of sight is fundamental good design.
The white bezels and pastel chin disappear in practice despite concerns, and the unadorned chin without an Apple logo feels right.
The choice of Touch ID over Face ID raises interesting questions about cost, design aesthetics, and the deliberateness required for authentication confirmations.
Apple's color coordination extends beyond the hardware to the OS itself, with a new 'This Mac' accent color option that matches the iMac's finish.
money_quotes: Computers need to look new. The 24-inch iMacs look new.
Is it a cheat to make the power supply external? Sure, maybe. But great designers know how to cheat right.
The right question is: Why should MacBooks and iOS devices be the only devices that look as thin as possible?
It's not a display with a computer behind it, it's a display with a computer below it.
Every other all-in-one PC — including the previous iMacs — looks bloated in comparison. Most dedicated displays look bloated in comparison.
Again, great designers — including chip designers — know how to cheat well.
In all the years I've been using a Mac, I've never been more tempted to stick with the default desktop background and accent/highlight colors.
The 24-inch M1 iMac is an iconic design that will stand the test of time for years to come.
key_insight: The M1 chip doesn't just deliver performance — it enables entirely new design possibilities, and the 24-inch iMac is the first Mac to fully exploit that potential by reimagining what a desktop computer should look like.
